title: The WHERE Clause
description: How to filter for exactly what you want.

slides.title: Finding a Needle 🪡
slides.content:
# We need a filter!

If Netflix wants to show you Action movies, they don't download their entire 10,000 movie database to your phone and then delete the non-action ones.

They ask the database to *only* send the Action movies in the first place!

slides.title: Enter WHERE 🛑
slides.content:
## Setting the rules

To filter rows, we use the incredibly powerful **`WHERE`** clause. It acts as a bouncer, only letting rows pass if they meet your condition.

`SELECT title FROM movies WHERE genre = 'Action';`

quizData.question: What is the primary purpose of the WHERE clause?

options.text: To filter which columns to show in the output
To filter which rows are retrieved based on a specific condition
To tell the database where to save a file

quizData.explanation: WHERE filters ROWS. SELECT filters COLUMNS.

slides.title: Single Quotes Matter 📜
slides.content:
## Text vs Columns

Notice how we put `'Action'` inside single quotes? 

In SQL, you must put **text values inside single quotes**. If you don't, SQL will think you are looking for a *column* named Action, and it will crash!

`WHERE genre = Action;` ❌ (Crash!)
`WHERE genre = 'Action';` ✅ (Success!)

quizData.question: Which WHERE clause correctly filters for users named John?
quizData.explanation: In standard SQL, actual text data (strings) must be wrapped in single quotes: 'John'. Double quotes are often reserved for column aliases.

slides.title: Give it a shot!
slides.content: Retrieve everything (`*`) from the `users` table where the `country` is exactly `'Canada'`.
